questions.question: What was the root cause of the memory leak in the authentication service and how was it fixed?
questions.expected_answer: The root cause was that the TokenCache class in auth_service/token_manager.py was not cleaning up expired tokens - the renew_token() method added new tokens to the cache dictionary but never removed old ones, causing unbounded memory growth. It was fixed by implementing an LRU cache with max size of 10,000 entries and adding a background task that runs every 5 minutes to remove expired tokens.
questions.reasoning: Multi-hop: (1) Architecture doc mentions the memory leak exists, (2) Sprint planning discusses the issue and assigns it, (3) Investigation doc reveals root cause and solution
required_connections: Identify that memory leak was mentioned in architecture overview
Find sprint planning discussion assigning investigation to Alex
Locate detailed investigation findings with root cause
Synthesize the technical details of the fix

questions.question: Why were file uploads timing out and what was the complete solution implemented?
questions.expected_answer: File uploads larger than 50MB were timing out because the data processing service had a hardcoded timeout of 60 seconds. Given the processing rates (1.2 seconds per MB for CSV to JSON), a 50MB file takes approximately 60 seconds, hitting the timeout limit exactly. The solution was to increase the timeout configuration to 300 seconds (5 minutes) in the data_processing/config.yaml file for file_upload, processing, and api_response operations.
questions.reasoning: Multi-hop: (1) Sprint planning mentions the problem (Jordan reported 50MB timeouts), (2) Timeout configuration doc explains the investigation, processing rates, and solution
required_connections: Identify timeout issue mentioned in sprint planning
Find decision to increase timeout to 300 seconds
Locate detailed investigation with processing rates
Connect the math: 50MB * 1.2s/MB ≈ 60s timeout limit
Synthesize the complete solution

questions.question: What is the current Redis endpoint and why was it migrated?
questions.expected_answer: The current Redis endpoint is redis-2.internal.local. It was migrated from redis-1.internal.local because redis-1 was experiencing high latency (avg 45ms) while redis-2 has better hardware (NVMe SSD vs SATA SSD) and shows average latency of only 8ms in testing. This resulted in an 80% reduction in cache latency and improved dashboard load times from 2.1s to 0.8s.
questions.reasoning: Multi-hop: (1) Sprint planning mentions redis-1.internal.local as current and potential migration to redis-2, (2) Infrastructure update doc details the completed migration with performance reasons
required_connections: Find mention of redis-1 in sprint planning
Locate migration completion in infrastructure update
Extract performance metrics and hardware differences
Synthesize the business justification

questions.question: If a user uploads a 100MB CSV file for JSON conversion, will it timeout? Show the calculation.
questions.expected_answer: No, it will not timeout. The data processing service has a timeout of 300 seconds (5 minutes). CSV to JSON conversion takes approximately 1.2 seconds per MB. Therefore, a 100MB CSV file would take approximately 100 * 1.2 = 120 seconds to process, which is well under the 300-second timeout limit. Testing confirmed a 100MB CSV file completed in 125 seconds.
questions.reasoning: Requires extracting multiple pieces of information from one document (timeout value, processing rate) and performing a calculation to answer
required_connections: Extract timeout value (300 seconds)
Extract CSV to JSON processing rate (1.2s per MB)
Perform calculation: 100MB * 1.2s/MB = 120s
Compare result to timeout limit
Note testing evidence

questions.question: A user reports that token renewals are slow. Based on the system changes, what might have improved performance and by how much?
questions.expected_answer: Token renewal performance was likely improved by the Redis migration from redis-1.internal.local to redis-2.internal.local on 2026-01-25. The migration reduced cache latency by 80% (from avg 45ms to 8ms). Since the authentication service uses Redis for caching (as documented in the architecture), and tokens are managed through the TokenCache class, token renewals would benefit from this improved cache performance. The overall system showed dashboard load time improvements from 2.1s to 0.8s after the migration.
questions.reasoning: Investigative: requires connecting architecture (Redis usage), token management implementation, and infrastructure changes to infer performance impact
required_connections: Identify that auth service uses Redis (architecture doc)
Identify that TokenCache manages token renewals (investigation doc)
Find Redis migration details and performance metrics
Infer that token renewal performance would improve from cache latency reduction
Quantify the improvement based on available metrics
